fields.headline: Scaled label revenue from five to seven figures a month
fields.deck: Turned the distribution and monetization system from a manual operation into an automated pipeline that kept growing without added headcount.

fields.headline: Helped 1,000+ creators and artists boost their revenue by up to 50%
fields.deck: Unlocked short-form monetization by pairing the label's audio catalog with creators' visual content on a revenue-share basis.

fields.headline: Launched promo campaigns reaching 100M+ views on average
fields.deck: Pushed the label's audio catalog into short-form content on two fronts: the publisher's own network of YouTube channels and revenue-share deals with external partners and creators — B2B and B2C alike.

fields.headline: Made a 100+ member support team 30% faster
fields.deck: Implemented operational guides and workflows for a 100+ member support team during merger-driven rapid product scaling.

fields.headline: Cut manual distribution work by 40%
fields.deck: Designed a roadmap for fully automated music distribution across 100+ streaming platforms on an enter-once, reuse-everywhere model.

fields.title: Career Break
fields.deck: Stepped away and autonomously built where my experience pointed — and watched the map open up with every project. What I found: my track record is a toolkit, not a destination. And the place where the whole toolkit works at once is music industry.
bullets: Built HackTube — a full-cycle production studio for BGM YouTube videos: find the idea, edit, render — inside YouTube's native UI, because the best onboarding is the one the user already finished.
Built MonkeyLearn — a coaching platform that turns skill-building into routine actions with clear instructions. When you can see the path, you don't lose the goal.

Launched and monetized a BGM YouTube channel in the Slow Life Piano niche, plus a series of smaller projects exploring what a modern artist's creative process needs today — from reels to community building.

fields.title: Head Of Product
fields.deck: Owned the product side of music production, distribution, and promotion. Leveraged automation to grow margins and keep creative focus on high-value work.
bullets: Researched and built an end-to-end music production, distribution, and monetization pipeline, boosting artist and creator revenue by up to 50%.
Consolidated dozens of third-party reports into a central visualization system to spot revenue anomalies and pattern shifts, assisting scaling from 5 to 7 figures.
Designed internal workflows and process frameworks to merge the teams and infrastructure of two major matrix organizations.

fields.title: Head of Label
fields.deck: Validated the unit economics of background music production and built the foundation to scale it into a repeatable revenue engine.
bullets: Developed and executed data-grounded monetization strategies for music as a standalone product and as video content assets.
Oversaw the buildout of IT infrastructure serving independent musicians and influencers on a revenue-share model.
Launched and scaled a network of YouTube promotion channels reaching a combined audience of 1M+.

fields.title: Label Manager
fields.deck: Ran A&R and licensing operations, signing independent artists and connecting their music with video projects across platforms.
bullets: Scouted and signed independent talent, managing legal contracts, licensing agreements, and promotional campaigns end-to-end.
Embedded catalog tracks into video projects, growing revenue on both sides — for the artists and the label.
Onboarded 200+ artists and tripled the label's music catalog at flat operating costs.

fields.title: Creative Producer
fields.deck: My first independent people and product management role — and my first English-speaking work environment. I did my best in that setting at that moment, and it kept teaching me long after the page was turned.
bullets: Hired and trained a 15+ production crew from scratch and ran it day to day.
Built the production workflow into a repeatable template — by the end, the team could take on almost any video production task within the publisher.
Launched a video project as an independent business unit across several platforms.

fields.title: Technical Lead
fields.deck: The filming teams' quality-of-life department — and the producer's right hand on day-to-day operations.
bullets: Wrote production guides and ran master classes for the filming crews — so the same lesson never had to be learned twice.
Researched the YouTube video ecosystem and our place in it, then turned the findings into a content loop: what the data liked, the next batch of videos got.
Oversaw the production loop end to end — shoot, edit, voice-over, translation, compilation — coaching each role along the way through revisions.

fields.title: Video Designer
fields.deck: My first video job — I came in green, fresh from doing legal support for real-estate deals, and spent a double-length probation being exactly average: the camera just isn't my instrument to play. But I needed to stay in the game, so I decided to close the gap with editing quality and speed.
bullets: Learned Premiere Pro from scratch, every hotkey written out on two A4 sheets I still sentimentally keep — then ran a master class where anyone could race me on the same source files, on a timer. Nobody was faster.
Recorded the company's internal video course on editing shortcuts — voice-over on screencast — that colleagues kept asking about long after.
Wrote, shot, and edit videos for the social media projects, learning every part of the craft on the job — some reached millions of views, some hundreds of millions.

fields.title: Master of Laws
fields.deck: Honest take: this degree had exactly one job — keeping me out of the army. And if I was buying myself two student years anyway, the braver me would have spent them on audio engineering, the old dream. I just didn't believe in myself enough to even see that door at that time. Still:
bullets: Practice showed me that law isn't my passion — in a music setting it kind of is, but as a supporting role, not the lead (a note from today).
Played the cards I had by a simple rule: better odds starting the search for myself in the creative field than in any legal job I was ready to see back then.
Graduated with honors.

fields.title: Bachelor of Laws
fields.deck: Landed in the right place at the right time, half by accident. Legal theory showed my curiosity what it had been looking for — how systems are built: structures, hierarchies, frameworks. It's always been tough for me to comprehend physics; the laws people wire into the essence of things turned out to be my way into systems thinking.
bullets: One seminar stuck with me: the Pussy Riot case was unfolding, and we spent the whole class playing the state — building the legal case for the prosecution while the professor took it apart point by point, showing us why the case was political. I didn't see this class this way back then, but do now.
My first real immersion into an environment that was genuinely interesting — just not my waters. I ran on default mode: executing for grades rather than for knowledge.
Graduated with honors.

/index.html.about.deck:
I help music and the people who make it earn what they deserve. 
Product guy with a proven record of turning data into monetization engines and scaling business revenue from five to seven figures monthly in the past. 
Now exploring how to be useful in the artist development game — with the skills that got me here, the ones I'm picking up along the way, and the personality that comes with them.
